results.possibleIdentity: Murray Bernstein was an engineer at Grumman Aircraft Engineering Corporation (later Grumman Aerospace and Northrop Grumman), primarily active from the 1960s through the early 2000s. Based in Bethpage, New York, he specialized in dynamic analysis, loads and dynamics, and vibration testing. During the late 1960s, he was a member of Grumman's Dynamic Analysis Section, where he co-authored technical reports on vibration design procedures for the Orbiting Astronomical Observatory (OAO). In the early 1970s, he played a key role in the analytical and experimental vibration studies of 1/8-scale models of the Space Shuttle Orbiter for NASA. His work involved reconciling test data with finite-element models, focusing on structural panels and joints. Bernstein's career continued at Northrop Grumman, where he contributed to the B-2 Aft Deck Team (which received a project award in 2005) and conducted analysis on high-speed data pods and structural tracking for aircraft flight sciences. His technical expertise in sensors and data acquisition for dynamic testing aligns with fields of electronics and communications in an aerospace context. He is also noted in archival records as a potential technical collaborator with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which facilitated partnerships between New York-based engineers and artists.
